items.q: Which of the 6 Rs has the GREATEST environmental benefit?
items.correct: Refuse or Reduce (preventing the impact at source).
items.why: Students treat recycling as the gold standard, but recycling uses energy and downgrades materials. The hierarchy is Refuse, Reduce, Reuse, Repair, Recycle, Rethink - PREVENTION beats reprocessing.

items.q: What force does a rope in a tug-of-war experience?
items.correct: Tension - it is being pulled at both ends.
items.misconception: Compression - because it is squeezed by hands.
items.why: Students confuse the localised hand grip with the force through the rope. Pulling stretches the rope - that's tension. Compression would be pushing the ends together (which a rope cannot resist).

items.q: What gives carbon fibre composite its strength?
items.correct: The fibres provide tensile strength; the resin matrix holds them in place and transfers loads.
items.misconception: The carbon fibres alone - the resin is just glue.
items.why: Students dismiss the matrix as filler. In reality the matrix is essential: it transfers loads between fibres and protects them. Loose carbon fibres without matrix have very little structural strength.

items.q: Which ISO standard governs Life Cycle Assessment principles?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: ISO 14040. “ISO 9001” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Whose 2002 Cradle to Cradle inspired circular design?
items.why: It's McDonough and Braungart. “Manzini and Vezzoli” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Approximately what embodied energy does aluminium have (MJ per kg)?
items.why: The real figure is 155 MJ/kg. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
